front: Name the three layers of a strong dev-tool motion: organic usage, team adoption, enterprise governance.
back: Organic usage proving real workflows; team adoption standardizing norms/budgets; enterprise governance partnering Security/IT—not random gatekeeping.

front: What does “make expansion feel inevitable” mean in practice?
back: Value was already proven in real workflows—then you help govern it responsibly.

front: List the four steps of a strong ROI story structure from the module.
back: (1) Baseline cost of engineering time; (2) measurable workflow lever; (3) conservative improvement hypothesis; (4) risk-adjusted implementation.

front: Why use ranges instead of false precision in ROI?
back: Credibility—buyers smell fantasy math; directional honesty beats fake decimals.

front: If a stat is promotional, how should a credible seller treat it?
back: As a hypothesis until validated in the account’s context.

front: What topics do security & compliance conversations cover—and what is your advantage?
back: Identity, access, data handling, retention, audit evidence; advantage is structured follow-up and source discipline.

front: Three rules for security conversations with a serious buyer?
back: Confirm claims against primary trust/security docs; involve specialists when needed; never bluff in front of a CISO.

front: MEDDPICC — Metrics in dev-tool translation: what kinds of metrics?
back: Throughput, incident rates, onboarding time, developer satisfaction signals—pick what the buyer owns.

front: Who is often the economic buyer—and when does CTO enter earlier?
back: Often engineering leadership; CTO earlier in strategic accounts.

front: Decision criteria for dev tools must include what two requirement types?
back: Security requirements and developer experience requirements—both matter.

front: What does “paper process” include for these deals?
back: Procurement, legal, data processing terms, vendor risk reviews.

front: Apply MEDDPICC without forcing a template—what attitude does the module endorse?
back: Use the lens where it fits the account; don’t read a checklist robotically—map to dev-tool reality.

front: What does ‘risk-adjusted implementation’ add to an ROI story?
back: Training, guardrails, and an evaluation plan the buyer can defend internally—answers ‘how do we adopt this safely?’

front: Which workflow lever travels well to engineering managers in an ROI conversation?
back: Onboarding ramp-time compression on the org’s most complex service—measurable and felt every quarter.

front: How should you handle ‘incident recovery time’ as a Cursor lever?
back: Acknowledge it as a real lever, ask how the org currently measures MTTR, and propose a measurement plan that fits their incident process.

front: When does developer satisfaction become real signal vs anecdote?
back: When it is captured in the org’s existing survey methodology with explicit baselines and comparison cohorts.

front: Why pair throughput with a quality metric in the ROI story?
back: Throughput alone is gameable (more PRs, lower quality)—pair with rework or cycle time to make the story hold up under scrutiny.

front: Two behaviors that distinguish a real champion in MEDDPICC.
back: They take introductions to other personas and they tell you when something isn’t landing—not just polite agreement.

front: How do you express ‘Identify Pain’ for a Cursor deal so it gets prioritized?
back: Name a specific workflow loss (review latency, onboarding ramp, multi-file refactor cost) tied to a measurable cost the buyer already feels.

front: How do you express ‘Implication’ so it lands with leadership?
back: ‘If this workflow doesn’t improve, here’s the named cost—slower releases, higher rework, harder hiring—projected over the next year.’

front: Why map the Decision Process explicitly?
back: Without it, deals end the quarter with ‘legal needs another two weeks’—every time. Map who decides what, in what order, by when.

front: What does it mean to ‘sequence the deal’ across stakeholders?
back: Decide which proof, in which order, lands with which stakeholder so the deal moves forward without contradicting itself.

front: When should you bring in a Cursor security specialist vs handle it yourself?
back: When the conversation reaches contractual data-handling terms a CISO is preparing to redline—escalation is credibility, not weakness.

front: Single behavior that converts grassroots usage into a governed standard?
back: Surface usage to leadership and partner with Security on a control framework—don’t hide it to ‘protect the champion.’

front: What builds long-term trust with an enterprise account, call by call?
back: Precise sourcing, honest uncertainty, structured ROI, and consistent stories across stakeholders—even when individual calls slow down.
